title: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders

items.subtopic: Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma) and Related Disorders | DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION
items.title: Differentiating Systemic Sclerosis Subtypes: dcSSc vs lcSSc
items.content: Systemic sclerosis can be categorized into diffuse cutaneous SSc (dcSSc) and limited cutaneous SSc (lcSSc), each with distinct clinical features, progression patterns, and complications. Understanding these differences is crucial for predicting disease course and tailoring management strategies.
keyPoints: dcSSc is characterized by extensive skin induration starting in the fingers and progressing to proximal limbs and trunk.
In dcSSc, early development of interstitial lung disease (ILD) and acute renal involvement are common.
lcSSc often begins with Raynaud's phenomenon, followed by sclerodactyly confined to fingers, distal limbs, and face; the trunk is typically spared.
CREST syndrome is a subset of lcSSc characterized by calcinosis cutis, Raynaud's phenomenon, esophageal dysmotility, sclerodactyly, and telangiectasia.
Visceral organ involvement in lcSSc tends to be more insidious with potential late complications like pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) and primary biliary cirrhosis.

items.subtopic: Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma) and Related Disorders | DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION
items.title: Recognizing SSc sine scleroderma: A Benign Subset of Systemic Sclerosis
items.content: Some patients exhibit Raynaud's phenomenon and serologic features typical of systemic sclerosis without detectable skin thickening, termed SSc sine scleroderma. This subset is generally considered to have a more benign course.
keyPoints: SSc sine scleroderma involves characteristic clinical and laboratory features of systemic sclerosis but lacks detectable skin thickening.
This subset is associated with a relatively benign disease course compared to other forms of systemic sclerosis.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma) and Related Disorders | DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION

items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology of Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)
items.content: Systemic sclerosis is a sporadic disease with increasing incidence worldwide. In the U.S., it affects 9–46 individuals per million annually, with an estimated 100,000 cases, potentially higher if broader criteria are applied. There's significant regional variation in incidence rates, influenced by case definitions, environmental factors, and genetic predispositions.
keyPoints: Incidence rate in the U.S.: 9–46 cases per million per year
Estimated U.S. cases: ~100,000
Regional variations in incidence rates
Influence of age, sex, ancestry on disease susceptibility
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE

items.title: Sex and Ancestry Influence on Systemic Sclerosis Risk and Presentation
items.content: Systemic sclerosis shows a strong female predominance with a 4.6:1 ratio, particularly in childbearing years. Black individuals have higher age-specific incidence rates and mortality. Genetic factors such as skewed X chromosome inactivation and sex hormones like estrogens contribute to this bias. Environmental exposures also play a role. First-degree relatives of SSc patients face a 13-fold increased risk.
keyPoints: Female-to-male ratio: 4.6:1
Higher incidence and mortality in black individuals
Influence of sex hormones and X chromosome inactivation on disease susceptibility
Increased risk for first-degree relatives (13-fold)
Peak age of onset in women: 65–74 years, earlier in black patients
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE

items.title: Genetic Factors in Systemic Sclerosis Susceptibility
items.content: The genetic contribution to systemic sclerosis (SSc) susceptibility is modest, with low disease concordance rates observed in monozygotic twins (4.7%) compared to other autoimmune diseases like rheumatoid arthritis (12.3%). However, the prevalence of first-degree relatives with SSc among patients is significantly higher than in the general population (1.6% vs 0.026%), indicating a genetic component.
keyPoints: Low disease concordance rate in monozygotic twins for SSc: 4.7%
Higher prevalence of first-degree relatives with SSc among patients: 1.6% compared to general population: 0.026%
Genetic studies highlight the role of autoimmunity as an initial trigger
Common genetic variants associated with B and T lymphocyte activation, interferon responses, and HLA class II haplotypes
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · GENETIC CONTRIBUTION TO DISEASE SUSCEPTIBILITY

items.title: Genetic Variants Linked to SSc Manifestations
items.content: Certain genetic loci are associated with specific manifestations or serologic subsets of systemic sclerosis, such as ILD (CTGF, CD226), PAH (TNIP1), scleroderma renal crisis (HLA-DRB1*11:04), and anticentromere antibodies (HLA-DPB1*05:01). These variants suggest altered immune regulation leading to increased susceptibility to autoimmunity.
keyPoints: Genetic loci associated with specific SSc manifestations
ILD linked to CTGF, CD226; PAH to TNIP1; scleroderma renal crisis to HLA-DRB1*11:04; anticentromere antibodies to HLA-DPB1*05:01
Common genetic variants also associated with other autoimmune disorders, indicating shared pathogenic pathways
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · GENETIC CONTRIBUTION TO DISEASE SUSCEPTIBILITY

items.subtopic: ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS AND OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Environmental and Occupational Exposures as Potential Triggers of Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)
items.content: Systemic sclerosis (SSc) etiology is largely unknown, with environmental factors playing a significant role. Various occupational exposures such as particulate silica, polyvinyl chloride, epoxy resins, welding fumes, organic solvents, and aromatic hydrocarbons are tentatively linked to SSc. These exposures may cause epigenetic modifications like DNA methylation and histone changes that alter gene expression. Additionally, certain drugs and radiation therapy have been implicated in triggering SSc-like illnesses.
keyPoints: Environmental factors likely play a major role in SSc etiology due to modest genetic contribution.

Occupational exposures linked with SSc include particulate silica (quartz), polyvinyl chloride, epoxy resins, welding fumes, organic solvents, and aromatic hydrocarbons such as paint thinners, toluene, xylene, trichloroethylene.
Epigenetic modifications from these exposures may drive altered cellular gene expression.
Drugs like bleomycin, pentazocine, cocaine, appetite suppressants linked with PAH, and immune checkpoint inhibitors (e.g., PD-1 blockers) can trigger SSc-like illnesses.
Radiation therapy for cancer is associated with de novo onset or exacerbation of preexisting SSc.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS AND OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS AND OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Pathogenesis of Systemic Sclerosis: Role of Epigenetic Modifications and Environmental Triggers
items.content: The pathogenesis of systemic sclerosis involves environmental insults triggering epigenetic modifications in genetically predisposed individuals. These changes lead to altered gene expression, contributing to the disease's clinical manifestations. The three key processes are diffuse microangiopathy, inflammation/autoimmunity, and visceral/vascular fibrosis affecting multiple organs. Autoimmunity and vascular reactivity appear early, while fibrosis occurs later.
keyPoints: Environmental insults in genetically predisposed individuals lead to epigenetic modifications driving SSc pathogenesis.
Three cardinal processes: diffuse microangiopathy, inflammation/autoimmunity, and visceral/vascular fibrosis.
Autoimmunity and vascular reactivity are early features; fibrosis occurs later.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS AND OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES | PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Raynaud's Phenomenon as an Early Indicator in Systemic Sclerosis
items.content: Raynaud's phenomenon is often the earliest clinical manifestation of systemic sclerosis (SSc), characterized by altered blood-flow response to cold challenge. It can be primary or secondary, with secondary Raynaud's associated with more severe vascular changes.
keyPoints: Raynaud's phenomenon is commonly the earliest clinical manifestation of SSc.
Primary Raynaud's disease is generally benign and nonprogressive.
Secondary Raynaud's in SSc can lead to irreversible structural changes, ischemic ulcers, necrosis, and amputation.
Genetic studies have identified ADRA2A as a candidate causative gene.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · MODELS OF DISEASE | MICROANGIOPATHY

items.title: Pathogenic Mechanisms of Microangiopathy in Systemic Sclerosis
items.content: Microangiopathy in systemic sclerosis involves endothelial cell injury triggered by various factors, leading to altered vascular function and structure. This results in impaired blood flow and tissue ischemia.
keyPoints: Endothelial cell injury disrupts vasodilatory (nitric oxide, prostacyclin) and vasoconstricting (endothelin-1) substances production.
Upregulation of ICAM-1 and other adhesion molecules occurs.
Enhanced permeability, leukocyte diapedesis, coagulation cascade activation, and impaired fibrinolysis are observed.
Endothelial-mesenchymal transition (EndoMT) contributes to fibroproliferative vasculopathy.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · MODELS OF DISEASE | MICROANGIOPATHY

items.title: Role of Endothelial-Mesenchymal Transition in Systemic Sclerosis Vasculopathy
items.content: EndoMT is implicated in the pathogenesis of systemic sclerosis vasculopathy, leading to fibroproliferative changes and microvessel rarefaction. This process involves endothelial cells transitioning into myofibroblasts.
keyPoints: EndoMT leads to accumulation of myofibroblasts in intima and media of arterioles and small arteries.
In capillaries, EndoMT results in loss of endothelial cells and fibrosis.
Nailfold capillaroscopy can reveal microvessel rarefaction due to EndoMT.
Widespread capillary loss and impaired vessel repair are hallmarks of SSc.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · MODELS OF DISEASE | MICROANGIOPATHY

items.title: Role of CXCL4 in Systemic Sclerosis
items.content: CXCL4 is a key chemokine implicated in the pathogenesis of systemic sclerosis (SSc), acting as both a proinflammatory and profibrotic agent. It binds to extracellular self-nucleic acids, facilitating their uptake and activation of endosomal TLRs, which promotes myofibroblast differentiation from stromal progenitor cells.
keyPoints: Elevated serum levels of CXCL4 can predict future disease progression in SSc patients.
CXCL4 induces proinflammatory and antiangiogenic activities mediated via type 2 cytokines and IL-6, contributing to a profibrotic monocyte phenotype.
Plasmacytoid dendritic cells and CXCL4 are potential therapeutic targets due to their crucial pathogenic roles in SSc.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · INFLAMMATION AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 1)

items.title: T-Cell Activation and Polarization in Systemic Sclerosis
items.content: In systemic sclerosis, T cells exhibit a TH2-polarized immune response, driven by dendritic cells. This polarization is characterized by the expression of chemokine receptors and α1 integrin on circulating T cells, enhancing their binding to endothelium and fibroblasts.
keyPoints: Activated T cells in SSc show restricted T-cell receptor signatures indicative of oligoclonal expansion.
TH2 cytokines (IL-4, IL-13, IL-33, TSLP) induce fibroblast activation, while TH1 cytokine IFN-γ has antifibrotic properties by blocking cytokine-mediated fibroblast activation.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · INFLAMMATION AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 1)

items.title: B-Cell Dysfunction in Systemic Sclerosis
items.content: Altered B-cell homeostasis and function play a significant role in the pathogenesis of systemic sclerosis, contributing to both inflammatory processes and autoantibody generation.
keyPoints: Circulating B cells in SSc show elevated CD19 and co-stimulatory molecules CD80 and CD86, indicating chronic activation.
Elevated serum levels of APRIL and BAFF are associated with the extent of skin and lung involvement in SSc.
B cells secrete IL-6, TGF-β, and other profibrotic cytokines, directly contributing to the inflammatory and fibrotic processes.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · INFLAMMATION AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 1)

items.title: Role of Neutrophils and Mast Cells in SSc Pathogenesis
items.content: Neutrophils in systemic sclerosis (SSc) secrete fibrogenic mediators like TGF-β and IL-6, release ROS, and form extracellular traps contributing to thrombotic and immune responses. Mast cells are attracted into the skin via PAI-1 signaling from keratinocytes, releasing fibrogenic mediators including TGF-β and activating fibroblasts directly through serpine 1.
keyPoints: Neutrophils secrete fibrogenic TGF-β and IL-6, release ROS, and form extracellular traps.
Mast cells are attracted via PAI-1 signaling from keratinocytes.
Mast cells release fibrogenic mediators including TGF-β and activate fibroblasts through serpine 1.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · INFLAMMATION AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 2) | FIBROSIS

items.title: Clinical Utility of SSc-Specific Autoantibodies
items.content: Circulating antinuclear antibodies (ANAs) are present in virtually all patients with SSc, even at early stages. SSc-specific autoantibodies have distinct immunofluorescence patterns and associations with unique disease phenotypes and HLA haplotypes, aiding in diagnosis and risk stratification.
keyPoints: ANAs detected in nearly all SSc patients, including early stages.
SSc-specific autoantibodies show strong associations with unique disease phenotypes and HLA haplotypes.
Autoantibodies have clinical utility for diagnosis and risk stratification.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · INFLAMMATION AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 2) | FIBROSIS

items.title: Mechanisms of Fibrosis in SSc
items.content: Fibrosis in SSc involves multiple organs and is characterized by the replacement of normal tissue with rigid connective tissue. Activated fibroblasts proliferate, migrate, secrete extracellular matrix molecules, and transdifferentiate into myofibroblasts. Persistent activation leads to maladaptive repair and pathologic fibrosis.
keyPoints: Fibrosis affects multiple organs, replacing normal tissue with rigid connective tissue.
Activated fibroblasts proliferate, migrate, secrete extracellular matrix molecules, and become myofibroblasts.
Persistent activation results in maladaptive repair and pathologic fibrosis.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · INFLAMMATION AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 2) | FIBROSIS

items.title: Understanding Skin Pathology in Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)
items.content: In systemic sclerosis, skin pathology is characterized by dermal thickening due to the accumulation of homogenized collagen bundles oriented parallel to the epithelium. This process involves atrophy of adnexal glands and replacement of periadnexal and intradermal white adipose tissue with collagen.
keyPoints: Dermal thickening is caused by broad bundles of homogenized collagen.
Adnexal glands become atrophic.
Periadnexal and intradermal white adipose tissue is replaced with collagen.
Established skin fibrosis shows absence of inflammation.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PATHOLOGY | SKIN | LUNGS

items.title: Lung Involvement in Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)
items.content: In systemic sclerosis, lung involvement is common and characterized by nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP) with variable interstitial fibrosis and mild chronic inflammation. As the disease progresses, interstitial fibrosis and vascular damage dominate, often coexisting within the same biopsy. Pulmonary hypertension in SSc is associated with intimal thickening of pulmonary arteries and fibrosis of alveolar septae.
keyPoints: Lung involvement is common in both limited and diffuse cutaneous subsets of SSc.
NSIP pattern: variable interstitial fibrosis, mild chronic inflammation (T lymphocytes, macrophages, eosinophils).
Progression leads to dominance of interstitial fibrosis and vascular damage.
Pulmonary hypertension is linked to intimal thickening of pulmonary arteries and alveolar septae fibrosis.
Lymphocytic bronchiolitis may involve the submucosa of terminal bronchioles.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PATHOLOGY | SKIN | LUNGS

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | KIDNEYS | HEART | PATHOLOGY IN OTHER ORGS
items.title: Recognizing Scleroderma Renal Crisis: A Life-Threatening Complication of Systemic Sclerosis
items.content: Scleroderma renal crisis (SRC) is a critical complication in systemic sclerosis characterized by acute fibrinoid necrosis of the afferent arterioles, leading to an onion-skin pattern and ischemic collapse of glomeruli. This condition mimics thrombotic microangiopathies with associated thrombosis, thrombocytopenia due to platelet consumption, and intravascular hemolysis.
keyPoints: SRC is a life-threatening acute complication in systemic sclerosis (SSc).
Pathology includes acute fibrinoid necrosis of afferent arterioles with onion-skin pattern intimal proliferation.
Ischemic collapse of glomeruli occurs, similar to thrombotic microangiopathies like atypical hemolytic-uremic syndrome.
Thrombosis and thrombocytopenia due to platelet consumption are observed.
Intravascular hemolysis is present.
Complement activation may be evident in kidney biopsies.
Extensive vascular thrombosis, glomerular collapse, and peritubular capillary deposits predict irreversible renal failure.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | KIDNEYS | HEART | PATHOLOGY IN OTHER ORGANS

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | KIDNEYS | HEART | PATHOLOGY IN OTHER ORGS
items.title: Cardiac Involvement in Systemic Sclerosis: Subclinical Pathology and Its Implications
items.content: Systemic sclerosis frequently involves subclinical cardiac pathology affecting the myocardium and pericardium. Characteristic arteriolar lesions include concentric intimal hypertrophy, luminal narrowing, patchy contraction band necrosis, loss of cardiac myocytes, and myocardial fibrosis due to microvascular involvement and ischemia-reperfusion injury. Fibrosis of the conduction system, particularly at the sinoatrial node, is common.
keyPoints: Subclinical cardiac pathology is prevalent in systemic sclerosis (SSc).
Arteriolar lesions include concentric intimal hypertrophy and luminal narrowing.
Patchy contraction band necrosis and loss of cardiac myocytes are observed.
Myocardial fibrosis results from microvascular involvement and ischemia-reperfusion injury.
Fibrosis at the sinoatrial node is common, affecting conduction.
Increased frequency of epicardial atherosclerotic coronary artery disease may occur in SSc.
Pericardial involvement with chronic inflammatory infiltrates and fibrinous exudates can lead to pericardial effusions.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | KIDNEYS | HEART | PATHOLOGY IN OTHER ORGANS

items.title: Understanding the Complexity of Systemic Sclerosis (SSc) Subsets and Overlap Syndromes
items.content: Systemic sclerosis is a complex disease with multiple endophenotypes associated with distinct autoantibodies. It can present as either diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis (dcSSc) or limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (lcSSc), each having unique clinical trajectories. Additionally, patients may exhibit overlap syndromes where SSc coexists with other autoimmune diseases such as polymyositis and Sjögren's syndrome.
keyPoints: SSc is a complex disease affecting multiple organs.
Patients can be stratified into diffuse (dcSSc) or limited cutaneous (lcSSc) subsets, but the disease includes multiple endophenotypes.
Unique endophenotypes are linked to specific autoantibodies with distinct and mutually exclusive specificities.
Overlap syndromes in SSc may include polymyositis, Sjögren's syndrome, polyarthritis, autoimmune liver disease, or systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · OVERVIEW | INITIAL CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Differential Initial Presentations of dcSSc and lcSSc
items.content: The initial clinical presentation differs significantly between diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis (dcSSc) and limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (lcSSc). In dcSSc, the progression from Raynaud's phenomenon to other manifestations is rapid, occurring over weeks to months. Early signs include soft tissue swelling and pruritus, evolving into fibrosis with skin induration and joint stiffness. Conversely, lcSSc has a more indolent course, with symptoms like GERD and digital ulcers appearing years after Raynaud's phenomenon.
keyPoints: In dcSSc, the interval from Raynaud's to other manifestations is weeks to months.
Early signs in dcSSc include soft tissue swelling, puffy fingers, pruritus, and progression to fibrosis with skin induration and joint stiffness.
The initial 4 years of dcSSc are critical for rapidly evolving lung and renal damage.
In lcSSc, the interval from Raynaud's to manifestations like GERD or digital ulcers can be years.
lcSSc rarely presents with scleroderma renal crisis or significant interstitial lung disease (ILD), but may develop pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) and overlap syndromes.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · OVERVIEW | INITIAL CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Distinguishing Primary vs. Secondary Raynaud's Phenomenon
items.content: Differentiating primary from secondary Raynaud's phenomenon is crucial due to the differing implications for patient management and prognosis. Primary Raynaud's disease typically lacks an underlying cause, has a family history of the condition, does not involve digital tissue necrosis or ulceration, and presents with a negative ANA test. In contrast, secondary Raynaud's often occurs at an older age, is more severe, and may lead to ischemic ulcers or loss of digits.
keyPoints: Primary Raynaud's: absence of underlying cause, family history, no digital necrosis/ulceration, negative ANA test
Secondary Raynaud's: occurs at older age, more severe episodes (frequent, prolonged, painful), ischemic ulcers, digit loss
Up to 5% of the general population has Raynaud's phenomenon
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · RAYNAUD'S PHENOMENON

items.title: Role of Nailfold Capillaroscopy in Diagnosing Raynaud's Phenomenon
items.content: Nailfold capillaroscopy is a valuable diagnostic tool for distinguishing between primary and secondary Raynaud's phenomenon. It involves visualizing nailbed cutaneous capillaries to identify characteristic patterns: evenly spaced parallel loops in primary Raynaud's versus distorted, widened, irregular loops with microhemorrhages and vascular dropout in secondary Raynaud's. This technique aids not only in differentiation but also in early diagnosis of systemic sclerosis (SSc).
keyPoints: Primary Raynaud's: evenly spaced parallel capillary loops
Secondary Raynaud's: distorted, widened irregular loops, microhemorrhages, vascular dropout
Nailfold capillaroscopy aids in early diagnosis of SSc
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · RAYNAUD'S PHENOMENON

items.title: Recognizing Systemic Sclerosis (SSc) Skin Manifestations
items.content: Systemic sclerosis is characterized by distinctive skin changes that help differentiate it from other connective tissue diseases. The hallmark feature is bilateral symmetrical skin thickening, which typically begins in the fingers and progresses proximally. Early signs may include diffuse tanning or hypopigmentation with a 'salt-and-pepper' appearance. Advanced stages lead to fixed flexion contractures, reduced hand mobility, and microstomia.
keyPoints: Bilateral symmetrical skin thickening distinguishes SSc from other connective tissue diseases.
Skin involvement starts in the fingers and progresses proximally.
Early manifestations may include diffuse tanning or hypopigmentation with a 'salt-and-pepper' appearance.
Advanced stages result in fixed flexion contractures, reduced hand mobility, and microstomia.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · SKIN FEATURES

items.title: Identifying Severe Complications of Systemic Sclerosis
items.content: In late-stage systemic sclerosis, skin becomes thin and atrophic with tethering to subcutaneous fat. Telangiectasia (2–20 mm in diameter) correlates with microvascular disease severity, including pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH). Chronic ulcerations are common on extensor surfaces and can lead to osteomyelitis. Calcinosis cutis occurs in up to 40% of patients, particularly those with anti-centromere antibody-positive IcSSc, leading to potential nerve compression and secondary infections.
keyPoints: Late-stage SSc skin is thin, atrophic, and tethered to subcutaneous fat.
Telangiectasia (2–20 mm) correlates with microvascular disease severity, including PAH.
Chronic ulcerations can lead to osteomyelitis.
Calcinosis cutis occurs in up to 40% of patients, often causing nerve compression and secondary infections.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · SKIN FEATURES

items.title: Interstitial Lung Disease in Systemic Sclerosis
items.content: ILD is a common pulmonary complication of SSc, with clinically significant ILD developing in 16–43% of patients. It progresses rapidly early in the disease course, often within the first 3-5 years.
keyPoints: Clinically significant ILD develops in 16–43% of SSc patients.
Rapid progression occurs early (within 1st 3-5 years), with FVC declining by up to 30% per year.
Risk factors include male sex, African-American race, diffuse skin involvement, severe gastroesophageal reflux, and presence of topoisomerase I autoantibodies.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PULMONARY FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis and Monitoring of ILD in SSc
items.content: HRCT is more sensitive than chest radiography for detecting early ILD. PFTs are essential for monitoring progression, showing restrictive patterns with FVC <70% predicted.
keyPoints: HRCT detects ILD in up to 65% of patients; characteristic findings include subpleural reticular linear opacities and ground-glass opacifications.
PFT typically shows a restrictive ventilatory defect: FVC <70%, FEV₁/FVC >0.8, reduced TLC, and D₁₀₀.
Extent of interstitial changes on HRCT predicts ILD progression and mortality.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PULMONARY FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension in SSc
items.content: PAH is a late complication of SSc, affecting 8–12% of patients. It is defined by specific hemodynamic criteria and has a poor prognosis if untreated.
keyPoints: PAH develops in 8–12% of SSc patients as a late complication.
Defined by mPAP >20 mmHg, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure ≤15 mmHg, and PVR >2.0 Wood units.
3-year survival <50% if untreated; risk factors include limited cutaneous disease and high numbers of cutaneous telangiectasia.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PULMONARY FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Screening and Diagnosis of Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH) in Systemic Sclerosis
items.content: Systemic sclerosis patients should be screened for PAH at initial evaluation and annually due to its asymptomatic early stages. Doppler echocardiography is used as a noninvasive screening method, with an estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure >40 mmHg or tricuspid regurgitation jet velocities >3 m/s suggesting PAH. Cardiac catheterization is required for confirmation and assessment.
keyPoints: Screening for PAH at initial evaluation and annually
Doppler echocardiography: estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure >40 mmHg or tricuspid regurgitation jet velocities >3 m/s suggests PAH
Cardiac catheterization required to confirm diagnosis, assess severity, rule out other causes, and provide prognostic parameters
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PULMONARY FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Prognosis and Monitoring of Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension in Systemic Sclerosis
items.content: Despite more favorable hemodynamics, the prognosis for PAH associated with systemic sclerosis is worse than idiopathic PAH due to frequent concurrence of interstitial lung disease (ILD) and cardiac involvement. Serum NT-proBNP levels correlate with PAH presence and severity but are not specific as they can also be elevated in other heart diseases.
keyPoints: Prognosis for SSc-associated PAH is worse than idiopathic PAH
Frequent concurrence of ILD and cardiac involvement affects prognosis
Serum NT-proBNP levels correlate with PAH presence and severity but are not specific
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PULMONARY FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: GI Tract Involvement in SSc: Prevalence and Impact
items.content: Gastrointestinal involvement is the most common internal organ manifestation of systemic sclerosis (SSc), affecting up to 90% of patients. This can significantly impact quality of life, malnutrition, and mortality.
keyPoints: GI tract involvement seen in up to 90% of SSc patients
Associated with male sex and specific autoantibodies
Pathologic findings include fibrosis, smooth-muscle atrophy, and obliterative small-vessel vasculopathy
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · GASTROINTESTINAL INVOLVEMENT

items.title: Upper GI Tract Involvement in SSc: GERD and Complications
items.content: Most SSc patients experience symptoms of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) such as heartburn, regurgitation, and dysphagia. Esophageal manometry often shows abnormal motility even without symptoms. Endoscopy is indicated for dysphagia to rule out infections.
keyPoints: Common GERD symptoms: heartburn, regurgitation, dysphagia
Esophageal manometry shows abnormal motility in most patients
Endoscopy may be needed for dysphagia to exclude Candida, herpes virus, and cytomegalovirus infections
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · GASTROINTESTINAL INVOLVEMENT

items.title: Lower GI Tract Involvement in SSc: Malnutrition and Diagnostic Challenges
items.content: Impaired intestinal motility and malabsorption lead to weight loss, malnutrition, and chronic diarrhea. Differentiating between pseudo-obstruction and mechanical obstruction is crucial for management.
keyPoints: Common issues include impaired motility, malabsorption, and bacterial overgrowth
Fat and protein malabsorption, vitamin B12 and D deficiency may occur
Differentiate pseudo-obstruction from mechanical obstruction for appropriate treatment
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · GASTROINTESTINAL INVOLVEMENT

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Scleroderma Renal Crisis
items.content: Scleroderma renal crisis is a severe complication of systemic sclerosis characterized by accelerated hypertension and acute kidney injury. It occurs in less than 15% of patients, typically within four years of disease onset. The pathogenesis involves obliterative vasculopathy leading to increased renin secretion and angiotensin II generation, causing a vicious cycle of renal vasoconstriction and hypertension. Risk factors include African-American race, male sex, diffuse skin involvement, and certain antibodies like anti-RNA polymerase III.
keyPoints: Occurs in <15% of SSc patients, usually within 4 years of onset
Accelerated hypertension (>150/90 mmHg) with progressive oliguric renal insufficiency
Risk factors: African-American race, male sex, diffuse skin involvement, anti-RNA polymerase III antibodies
Palpable tendon friction rubs, pericardial effusion, new unexplained anemia, thrombocytopenia as harbingers
Daily blood pressure monitoring recommended for high-risk early-stage SSc patients
Glucocorticoid use should be limited (<10 mg/d) in high-risk patients
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · RENAL INVOLVEMENT: SCLERODERMA RENAL CRISIS

items.title: Diagnostic and Prognostic Considerations in Scleroderma Renal Crisis
items.content: Scleroderma renal crisis can be misdiagnosed as thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) due to overlapping features like microangiopathic hemolysis. Key diagnostic indicators include moderate thrombocytopenia, fragmented red blood cells, mild proteinuria, granular casts, and microscopic hematuria. A creatinine level >3 mg/dL at presentation predicts poor outcomes such as permanent hemodialysis and mortality. Renal biopsy showing vascular thrombosis or glomerular ischemic collapse also indicates a worse prognosis.
keyPoints: Can be misdiagnosed as TTP; renal biopsy may help differentiate
Characterized by moderate thrombocytopenia, fragmented red blood cells, mild proteinuria, granular casts, microscopic hematuria
Creatinine >3 mg/dL at presentation predicts poor outcome (permanent hemodialysis, mortality)
Vascular thrombosis and glomerular ischemic collapse on biopsy indicate worse prognosis
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · RENAL INVOLVEMENT: SCLERODERMA RENAL CRISIS

items.title: Cardiac Involvement in SSc: Detection and Impact
items.content: Cardiac involvement is detected in 10–50% of systemic sclerosis (SSc) patients when screened with sensitive diagnostic tools. It is more frequent in diffuse cutaneous SSc (dcSSc) than limited cutaneous SSc (lcSSc), often leading to poor outcomes due to its association with primary or secondary complications like pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH). Manifestations include pericarditis, effusions, constrictive pericarditis, and arrhythmias. Advanced imaging techniques such as cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (cMRI) and tissue Doppler echocardiography (TDE) are crucial for detecting myocardial dysfunction.
keyPoints: Cardiac involvement detected in 10–50% of SSc patients with sensitive tools
More frequent in dcSSc than lcSSc
Associated with poor outcomes
Manifestations: pericarditis, effusions, constrictive pericarditis, arrhythmias
Advanced imaging (cMRI, TDE) crucial for detection

items.title: Musculoskeletal Complications in SSc: Clinical Features and Risks
items.content: Musculoskeletal complications are common in systemic sclerosis (SSc), with carpal tunnel syndrome often presenting early. Joint mobility is progressively impaired, leading to fixed contractures at the proximal interphalangeal joints and wrists. Large joint contractures and tendon friction rubs, characterized by coarse crepitation, indicate increased risk for renal and cardiac complications and reduced survival. Muscle weakness in SSc is multifactorial, involving deconditioning, disuse atrophy, malnutrition, inflammation, and fibrosis. Acro-osteolysis and mandibular condyle resorption are notable skeletal changes.
keyPoints: Carpal tunnel syndrome often presents early
Progressive impairment of joint mobility leading to contractures
Tendon friction rubs indicate increased risk for renal/cardiac complications
Muscle weakness multifactorial: deconditioning, disuse atrophy, malnutrition, inflammation, fibrosis
Acro-osteolysis and mandibular condyle resorption are notable skeletal changes

items.subtopic: LESS RECOGNIZED DISEASE MANIFESTATIONS | LABORATORY EVALUATION AND BIOMARKERS
items.title: Recognizing Paraneoplastic Syndromes in SSc
items.content: Systemic sclerosis (SSc) can present with malignancies such as breast, lung, and ovarian carcinomas and lymphomas shortly after diagnosis. This association is particularly noted in patients positive for autoantibodies to RNA polymerase III, suggesting a paraneoplastic syndrome triggered by the antitumor immune response.
keyPoints: Increased risk of malignancies like breast, lung, and ovarian cancers, and lymphomas in SSc.
Temporal association with onset of SSc, especially in patients positive for RNA polymerase III antibodies.
SSc may represent a paraneoplastic syndrome triggered by antitumor immune response.

items.title: Identifying Scleroderma Renal Crisis and Associated Risks
items.content: Scleroderma renal crisis is characterized by microangiopathic hemolytic anemia due to mechanical fragmentation of red blood cells. It's associated with antibodies to RNA polymerase III, indicating a higher risk for this complication in SSc patients.
keyPoints: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia as a hallmark of scleroderma renal crisis.
Association with RNA polymerase III antibodies indicates increased risk.
Elevated serum IL-6 and CRP levels are linked to worse outcomes.

items.title: Diagnosing Systemic Sclerosis (SSc) with High Specificity and Sensitivity
items.content: The diagnosis of systemic sclerosis is primarily clinical. Diagnostic criteria are >90% specific and sensitive for SSc, making them highly reliable. Key features include skin induration with a characteristic symmetric distribution pattern and typical visceral organ manifestations.
keyPoints: Diagnostic criteria are >90% specific and sensitive for SSc.
Skin induration with characteristic symmetric distribution is crucial for diagnosis.
Visceral organ manifestations confirm the diagnosis.

items.title: Challenges and Strategies in Early Diagnosis of SSc
items.content: Early-stage systemic sclerosis can be challenging to diagnose due to nonspecific initial symptoms. In dcSSc, Raynaux's phenomenon may be absent, with only upper extremity edema and puffy fingers observed. Misdiagnosis as other conditions like arthritis or undifferentiated connective tissue disease is common.
keyPoints: Early-stage SSc diagnosis can be challenging due to nonspecific symptoms.
Raynaud's phenomenon may be absent in early dcSSc.
Misdiagnosis with conditions such as arthritis, SLE, myositis, or undifferentiated connective tissue disease is common.

items.title: Glucocorticoids in SSc: Use with Caution
items.content: Glucocorticoids can alleviate stiffness and aching in early inflammatory-stage dcSSc but do not affect the progression of skin or internal organ involvement. Their use is associated with an increased risk of scleroderma renal crisis, thus they should be used sparingly.
keyPoints: Alleviate stiffness and aching in early inflammatory-stage dcSSc
Do not influence progression of skin or internal organ involvement
Increased risk of scleroderma renal crisis; use only when necessary at the lowest dose for brief periods

items.subtopic: DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES: IMMUNOMODULATORY AGENTS | TABLE 372-7 Key Principles in Management
items.title: Cyclophosphamide's Role and Risks in SSc
items.content: Cyclophosphamide can reduce the progression of SSc-associated ILD, with stabilization or modest improvement in pulmonary function, HRCT findings, respiratory symptoms, and skin induration. However, its potential toxicity includes bone marrow suppression, opportunistic infections, hemorrhagic cystitis, bladder cancer, premature ovarian failure, and late secondary malignancies.
keyPoints: Reduces progression of SSc-associated ILD
Improves pulmonary function, HRCT findings, respiratory symptoms, skin induration
Potential toxicity: bone marrow suppression, opportunistic infections, hemorrhagic cystitis, bladder cancer, premature ovarian failure, late secondary malignancies

items.title: Mycophenolate Mofetil and Tocilizumab for SSc
items.content: Mycophenolate mofetil improves both skin induration and ILD, with good tolerance. Tocilizumab shows benefit on skin and lung involvement in early disease stages of SSc-associated ILD and is well tolerated.
keyPoints: Mycophenolate mofetil: Improves skin induration and ILD, well tolerated
Tocilizumab: Benefits skin and lung involvement in early SSc-ILD, well tolerated

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF SSc-ASSOCIATED INTERSTITIAL LUNG DISEASE | TREATMENT OF GASTROINTESTINAL COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Management of SSc-associated ILD: Monitoring and Immunotherapy
items.content: ILD is a leading cause of death in SSc patients. Identifying high-risk individuals involves assessing HRCT, FVC at initial evaluation, and decline over 12 months. Risk factors include male sex, older age, anti-topoisomerase I positivity, progressive skin involvement, and myocardial disease. High-risk patients should undergo PFTs every 3–6 months; serial HRCT is not recommended. Cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate mofetil are used to slow lung function decline and improve symptoms, with the latter preferred due to fewer side effects. Nintedanib may be added for ILD progression despite immunotherapy.
keyPoints: ILD is a leading cause of death in SSc patients.
Risk factors: male sex, older age, anti-topoisomerase I positivity, progressive skin involvement, myocardial disease.
Monitor high-risk patients with PFTs every 3–6 months; avoid serial HRCT.
Cyclophosphamide (monthly IV or oral for 6–12 months) and mycophenolate mofetil slow lung function decline.
Mycophenolate mofetil is preferred due to fewer side effects.
Nintedanib may be added if ILD progresses despite immunotherapy.

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF SSc-ASSOCIATED INTERSTITIAL LUNG DISEASE | TREATMENT OF GASTROINTESTINAL COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Approach to Gastrointestinal Complications in SSc: Reflux and Dysmotility Management
items.content: Oral care is crucial due to common issues like decreased oral aperture and saliva production. GERD is prevalent even without symptoms; management includes head-of-bed elevation, small meals, avoiding alcohol/caffeine, and high-dose proton pump inhibitors. Prokinetic agents (erythromycin, prucalopride, domperidone) may help but have side effects. Botulinum toxin can improve gastric emptying. Avoid surgical antireflux procedures due to risk of secondary achalasia. Treat watermelon stomach with endoscopic laser or argon plasma photocoagulation. Enteral feeding/decompression via gastrostomy/jejunostomy may be necessary for severe cases. Address small intestinal bacterial overgrowth with rotating antibiotics (metronidazole, erythromycin, rifaximin). Manage fecal incontinence with antidiarrheals, biofeedback, sphincter augmentation, and sacral neuromodulation.
keyPoints: Regular dental care is recommended for oral issues.
GERD management: elevate head of bed, small meals, avoid alcohol/caffeine, high-dose proton pump inhibitors.
Prokinetic agents (erythromycin, prucalopride, domperidone) may help but have side effects.
Botulinum toxin can improve gastric emptying; avoid surgical antireflux procedures.
Treat watermelon stomach with endoscopic laser or argon plasma photocoagulation.
Enteral feeding/decompression via gastrostomy/jejunostomy for severe cases.
Use rotating antibiotics (metronidazole, erythromycin, rifaximin) for small intestinal bacterial overgrowth.
Manage fecal incontinence with antidiarrheals, biofeedback, sphincter augmentation, sacral neuromodulation.

items.title: Screening and Treatment Strategies for PAH in SSc Patients
items.content: Patients with systemic sclerosis (SSc) should be screened for pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) at initial evaluation and annually due to its asymptomatic nature until advanced stages. Treatment typically begins with oral endothelin-1 receptor antagonists like bosentan or phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors such as sildenafil. Additional treatments include soluble guanylate cyclase stimulators, selective IP prostacyclin receptor agonists, diuretics, digoxin, and supplemental oxygen if hypoxemia is present. Combination therapy may be necessary for optimal outcomes.
keyPoints: PAH accounts for 30% of deaths in SSc patients.
Screening should occur at initial evaluation and yearly thereafter.
Treatment options include bosentan, sildenafil, riociguat, selexipag, diuretics, digoxin, and supplemental oxygen if needed.
Combination therapy is often necessary.
Lung transplantation has a 2-year survival rate of 64%, comparable to idiopathic ILD or PAH.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · TREATMENT OF PULMONARY ARTERIAL HYPERTENSION | TREATMENT OF RENAL CRISIS

items.title: Management and Prognosis of Scleroderma Renal Crisis
items.content: Scleroderma renal crisis is a medical emergency requiring prompt recognition and treatment. High-risk patients should monitor blood pressure daily. Treatment involves titration of short-acting ACE inhibitors to rapidly normalize blood pressure, with additional medications considered for persistent hypertension. Eculizumab may be added due to intrarenal complement pathway activation in some cases. Up to two-thirds of patients may need dialysis, but substantial renal recovery is possible, allowing discontinuation of renal replacement therapy in 30–50% of patients. Kidney transplantation is an option after 2 years if dialysis cannot be discontinued.
keyPoints: Scleroderma renal crisis requires immediate hospitalization and treatment with ACE inhibitors.
High-risk patients should monitor blood pressure daily.
Up to two-thirds may necessitate dialysis, but 30–50% can discontinue renal replacement therapy.
Kidney transplantation is appropriate after 2 years if dialysis persists.
Survival post-transplantation is comparable to other diseases with rare recurrence of renal crisis.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · TREATMENT OF PULMONARY ARTERIAL HYPERTENSION | TREATMENT OF RENAL CRISIS

items.subtopic: SKIN CARE | TREATMENT OF MUSCULOSKELETAL COMPLICATIONS | COURSE
items.title: Management of Skin Involvement in Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)
items.content: Skin involvement in SSc is not life-threatening and may regress spontaneously. Management focuses on symptom control rather than aggressive treatment, using antihistamines, low-dose glucocorticoids (<5 mg/d of prednisone), hydrophilic ointments, bath oils, skin massage, and specific treatments for complications like telangiectasia and ischemic digital ulcerations.
keyPoints: Skin involvement in SSc is not life-threatening and may regress spontaneously.
Use antihistamines and low-dose glucocorticoids (<5 mg/d of prednisone) for early inflammatory symptoms.
Hydrophilic ointments, bath oils, and regular skin massage are encouraged.
Telangiectasia can be treated with pulsed dye laser.
Ischemic digital ulcerations should be protected by exclusive dressings to promote healing and prevent infection.
Infected ulcers require topical antibiotics and surgical debridement.
Minocycline, bisphosphonates, and sodium thiosulfate (STS) may help with soft tissue calcific depositions.

items.title: Course and Prognosis of Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)
items.content: The course of SSc is highly variable, with dcSSc having a more rapidly progressive course than lcSSc. Inflammatory symptoms in early dcSSc subside as skin thickening plateaus at 2–4 years after onset. Visceral organ involvement may develop during the early stage and progress even after skin involvement peaks. Scleroderma renal crisis typically occurs within the first 4 years, while lcSSc often presents with Raynaud's phenomenon preceding other symptoms.
keyPoints: The course of SSc is highly variable; dcSSc has a more rapidly progressive course than lcSSc.
Inflammatory symptoms in early dcSSc subside as skin thickening plateaus at 2–4 years after onset.
Visceral organ involvement may develop during the early stage and progress even after skin involvement peaks.
Scleroderma renal crisis generally occurs within the first 4 years of disease.
In lcSSc, Raynaud's phenomenon typically precedes other symptoms by years or decades.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · SKIN CARE | TREATMENT OF MUSCULOSKELETAL COMPLICATIONS | COURSE

items.title: Survival Rates and Predictors in Systemic Sclerosis (SSc)
items.content: Systemic sclerosis significantly increases the risk of premature death, with age- and gender-adjusted mortality rates fivefold to eightfold higher than the general population. Survival varies between diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis (dcSSc) and limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (lcSSc), with median survival at 11 years. Key predictors of poor prognosis include male gender, African ancestry, older age at onset, extensive skin involvement, palpable tendon friction rubs, and significant visceral organ involvement.
keyPoints: Age- and gender-adjusted mortality rates are fivefold to eightfold higher than the general population.
Median survival in SSc is 11 years.
5-year and 10-year survival for dcSSc: 70% and 55%, respectively.
5-year and 10-year survival for lcSSc: 90% and 75%, respectively.
Prognosis correlates with extent of skin involvement, a surrogate for visceral organ involvement.
Major causes of death include PAH, pulmonary fibrosis, GI involvement, cardiac disease, and scleroderma renal crisis (30% 3-year mortality).
Markers of poor prognosis: male gender, African ancestry, older age at onset, extensive skin thickening with truncal involvement, palpable tendon friction rubs.
Laboratory predictors of increased mortality: elevated ESR, anemia, proteinuria, anti-topoisomerase I antibodies.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PROGNOSIS

items.title: Impact of PAH and Renal Crisis on SSc Prognosis
items.content: The severity of pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) is a critical predictor of mortality in systemic sclerosis, with patients having a mean pulmonary arterial pressure ≥45 mmHg experiencing only 33% 3-year survival. The introduction of ACE inhibitors has dramatically improved outcomes for scleroderma renal crisis, increasing 1-year survival from <10% to >70% 3-year survival. Overall, the 10-year survival rate in SSc has improved significantly over decades due to earlier detection and better management.
keyPoints: Severity of PAH predicts mortality; mean pulmonary arterial pressure ≥45 mmHg correlates with 33% 3-year survival.
Scleroderma renal crisis associated with 30% 3-year mortality.
ACE inhibitors have improved scleroderma renal crisis survival from <10% at 1 year to >70% at 3 years.
10-year survival in SSc has increased from <60% in the 1970s to >66–78% in the 1990s.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PROGNOSIS

items.title: Localized Scleroderma: Recognizing Morphea and Linear Scleroderma
items.content: Localized scleroderma, unlike systemic sclerosis (SSc), typically does not involve Raynaud's phenomenon or significant internal organ involvement. It presents in children more frequently than adults. Morphea manifests as solitary or multiple circular patches of thickened skin, while linear scleroderma can lead to fibrosis and atrophy affecting subcutaneous tissues, tendons, muscles, and bones.
keyPoints: Localized scleroderma occurs more commonly in children.
Morphea presents as circular patches or widespread induration (generalized or pansclerotic morphea).
Linear scleroderma can affect subcutaneous tissues, leading to fibrosis and atrophy of supporting structures, tendons, muscle, and bone.
In children, linear scleroderma may slow the growth of affected long bones.
Significant contractures can develop if linear scleroderma crosses large joints.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · LOCALIZED SCLERODERMA | MIXED CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE

items.title: Mixed Connective Tissue Disease: Initial Presentation and Progression
items.content: Mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD) involves features of lcSSc, SLE, polymyositis, and rheumatoid arthritis. It is associated with high titers of autoantibodies to U1-RNP. The initial presentation often includes Raynaud's phenomenon, puffy fingers, and myalgia. Over time, patients may develop sclerodactyly, soft tissue calcinosis, cutaneous telangiectasia, and skin rashes suggestive of SLE.
keyPoints: MCTD is associated with high titers of autoantibodies to U1-RNP.
Initial presentation includes Raynaud's phenomenon, puffy fingers, and myalgia.
Over time, patients may develop sclerodactyly, soft tissue calcinosis, cutaneous telangiectasia.
Skin rash suggestive of SLE (malar erythema, photosensitivity) can occur.

items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · RAYNAUD'S PHENOMENON

items.statement: Secondary Raynaud's phenomenon typically presents with less severe symptoms compared to primary Raynaud's disease.
items.explanation: Secondary Raynaud's tends to occur at an older age, is more severe (episodes more frequent, prolonged, and painful), and can be complicated by ischemic digital ulcers.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · RAYNAUD'S PHENOMENON

items.statement: The number of telangiectasias in systemic sclerosis correlates with the severity of microvascular disease.
items.explanation: Telangiectasia numbers correlate with microvascular disease severity, including PAH.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · SKIN FEATURES

items.statement: Calcinosis cutis in systemic sclerosis is associated with elevated serum calcium and phosphate levels.
items.explanation: Calcinosis occurs despite normal serum calcium and phosphate levels.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · SKIN FEATURES

items.statement: Interstitial lung disease in systemic sclerosis is most rapidly progressive after the first 3–5 years of the disease.
items.explanation: The most rapid progression of ILD generally occurs early in the disease course, within the first 3–5 years.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PULMONARY FEATURES (part 1)

items.statement: Mutations in the BMPR2 gene are associated with systemic sclerosis-associated pulmonary arterial hypertension.
items.explanation: Mutations in the BMPR2 gene implicated in idiopathic PAH are not associated with SSc-PAH.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PULMONARY FEATURES (part 1)

items.statement: Serum levels of NT-proBNP are specific for diagnosing PAH in systemic sclerosis patients.
items.explanation: Elevated levels of NT-proBNP occur in other forms of right and left heart disease, not just PAH.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PULMONARY FEATURES (part 2)

items.statement: Cardiac catheterization is necessary to confirm the diagnosis of suspected PAH.
items.explanation: Cardiac catheterization confirms the diagnosis and assesses severity in suspected PAH.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PULMONARY FEATURES (part 2)

items.statement: Barium contrast studies are sensitive and specific for evaluating gastric involvement in SSc.
items.explanation: The passage states that barium contrast studies are neither sensitive nor specific for evaluation of gastric involvement.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · GASTROINTESTINAL INVOLVEMENT

items.statement: Patients with SSc have an increased prevalence of Helicobacter pylori infection in the stomach.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that patients with SSc show an increased prevalence of Helicobacter pylori infection.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · GASTROINTESTINAL INVOLVEMENT

items.statement: Scleroderma renal crisis can be the presenting manifestation of systemic sclerosis.
items.explanation: The passage states that scleroderma renal crisis can occasionally even be the presenting manifestation of SSc.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · RENAL INVOLVEMENT: SCLERODERMA RENAL CRISIS

items.statement: Scleroderma renal crisis is more common in patients with anti-centromere antibodies.
items.explanation: Patients with anti-centromere antibodies appear protected from scleroderma renal crisis.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · RENAL INVOLVEMENT: SCLERODERMA RENAL CRISIS

items.statement: Musculoskeletal complications in systemic sclerosis can include erosive polyarthritis.
items.explanation: Occasional SSc patients develop erosive polyarthritis in the hands.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · CARDIAC INVOLVEMENT | MUSCULOSKELETAL COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: Muscle weakness in systemic sclerosis is primarily due to inflammation.
items.explanation: Muscle weakness is multifactorial, including deconditioning, disuse atrophy, malnutrition, inflammation, and fibrosis.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · CARDIAC INVOLVEMENT | MUSCULOSKELETAL COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: LESS RECOGNIZED DISEASE MANIFESTATIONS | LABORATORY EVALUATION AND BIOMARKERS
items.statement: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia in SSc is caused by mechanical fragmentation of red blood cells due to microvessel coating with fibrin or platelet thrombi.
items.explanation: This condition is a hallmark of scleroderma renal crisis.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · LESS RECOGNIZED DISEASE MANIFESTATIONS | LABORATORY EVALUATION AND BIOMARKERS

items.statement: The erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is typically elevated in SSc.
items.explanation: In SSc, the ESR is generally normal; an elevation may indicate coexisting myositis or malignancy.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · LESS RECOGNIZED DISEASE MANIFESTATIONS | LABORATORY EVALUATION AND BIOMARKERS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS, STAGING, AND MONITORING | OVERVIEW: GENERAL PRINCIPLES
items.statement: The diagnosis of systemic sclerosis is primarily based on laboratory tests.
items.explanation: Diagnosis of SSc is made primarily on clinical grounds and not solely on laboratory tests.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS, STAGING, AND MONITORING | OVERVIEW: GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS, STAGING, AND MONITORING | OVERVIEW: GENERAL PRINCIPLES
items.statement: Screening for interstitial lung disease (ILD) is recommended for patients with a new diagnosis of systemic sclerosis.
items.explanation: Patients with a new diagnosis of SSc should be screened for ILD and followed regularly.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS, STAGING, AND MONITORING | OVERVIEW: GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.subtopic: DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES: IMMUNOMODULATORY AGENTS | TABLE 372-7 Key Principles in Management
items.statement: Glucocorticoids are recommended for long-term use in SSc due to their efficacy.
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids should be used only when necessary, at the lowest dose possible, and for brief periods.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES: IMMUNOMODULATORY AGENTS | TABLE 372-7 Key Principles in Management

items.subtopic: DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES: IMMUNOMODULATORY AGENTS | TABLE 372-7 Key Principles in Management
items.statement: Nintedanib has shown benefit in slowing lung function decline in patients with SSc-ILD.
items.explanation: Clinical trials show nintedanib benefits by significantly slowing lung function decline in SSc-ILD.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES: IMMUNOMODULATORY AGENTS | TABLE 372-7 Key Principles in Management

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF SSc-ASSOCIATED INTERSTITIAL LUNG DISEASE | TREATMENT OF GASTROINTESTINAL COMPLICATIONS
items.statement: Recurrence of SSc-ILD in transplanted lung allografts has been reported.
items.explanation: Recurrence of SSc-ILD in transplanted lung allografts has not been reported.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · TREATMENT OF SSc-ASSOCIATED INTERSTITIAL LUNG DISEASE | TREATMENT OF GASTROINTESTINAL COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF SSc-ASSOCIATED INTERSTITIAL LUNG DISEASE | TREATMENT OF GASTROINTESTINAL COMPLICATIONS
items.statement: Prokinetic agents such as erythromycin are frequently associated with side effects in treating gastrointestinal complications of systemic sclerosis.
items.explanation: Prokinetic agents like erythromycin are indeed frequently associated with side effects.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · TREATMENT OF SSc-ASSOCIATED INTERSTITIAL LUNG DISEASE | TREATMENT OF GASTROINTESTINAL COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF PULMONARY ARTERIAL HYPERTENSION | TREATMENT OF RENAL CRISIS
items.statement: Prostacyclin analogues can be administered through continuous IV or SC infusion.
items.explanation: Prostacyclin analogues such as epoprostenol can indeed be given by continuous IV or SC infusion.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · TREATMENT OF PULMONARY ARTERIAL HYPERTENSION | TREATMENT OF RENAL CRISIS

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF PULMONARY ARTERIAL HYPERTENSION | TREATMENT OF RENAL CRISIS
items.statement: Kidney transplantation is inappropriate for patients unable to discontinue dialysis after 2 years.
items.explanation: Kidney transplantation is appropriate for patients who cannot discontinue dialysis after 2 years.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · TREATMENT OF PULMONARY ARTERIAL HYPERTENSION | TREATMENT OF RENAL CRISIS

items.subtopic: SKIN CARE | TREATMENT OF MUSCULOSKELETAL COMPLICATIONS | COURSE
items.statement: In systemic sclerosis, new visceral organ involvement is common even after the peak of skin involvement.
items.explanation: New organ involvement is rare once existing visceral organ involvement has peaked.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · SKIN CARE | TREATMENT OF MUSCULOSKELETAL COMPLICATIONS | COURSE

items.subtopic: SKIN CARE | TREATMENT OF MUSCULOSKELETAL COMPLICATIONS | COURSE
items.statement: The natural history of systemic sclerosis can be highly variable and difficult to predict, especially in early stages.
items.explanation: The course of SSc is indeed highly variable and unpredictable in the early stages.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · SKIN CARE | TREATMENT OF MUSCULOSKELETAL COMPLICATIONS | COURSE

items.statement: The median survival for patients with SSc is approximately 11 years.
items.explanation: One population-based study reported a median survival of 11 years in SSc.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PROGNOSIS

items.statement: Lung cancer does not contribute to increased mortality in systemic sclerosis (SSc).
items.explanation: Lung cancer is one of the factors contributing to increased mortality in SSc.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: LOCALIZED SCLERODERMA | MIXED CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE
items.statement: Morphea can present as widespread induration known as generalized or pansclerotic morphea.
items.explanation: Morphea may rarely present as widespread induration, termed generalized or pansclerotic morphea.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · LOCALIZED SCLERODERMA | MIXED CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE

items.subtopic: LOCALIZED SCLERODERMA | MIXED CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE
items.statement: Linear scleroderma typically spares the fingers from involvement.
items.explanation: In linear scleroderma, the fingers are generally spared.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · LOCALIZED SCLERODERMA | MIXED CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE

items.subtopic: Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma) and Related Disorders | DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION
items.assertion: The CREST syndrome is associated with limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis.
items.reason: CREST syndrome includes calcinosis cutis, Raynaud's phenomenon, esophageal dysmotility, sclerodactyly, and telangiectasia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why CREST syndrome is associated with limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma) and Related Disorders | DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION

items.assertion: Endothelial cell injury in systemic sclerosis is triggered by various factors.
items.reason: Viruses, cytotoxic factors, and autoantibodies are considered possible triggers of endothelial cell injury.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · MODELS OF DISEASE | MICROANGIOPATHY

items.assertion: Revascularization is effective in restoring blood flow to ischemic tissue in systemic sclerosis.
items.reason: The process of revascularization is defective despite elevated levels of angiogenic factors.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason correctly states that revascularization is defective.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · MODELS OF DISEASE | MICROANGIOPATHY

items.assertion: Plasmacytoid dendritic cells are potential therapeutic targets in SSc.
items.reason: They secrete CXCL4, which has crucial pathogenic roles in the disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · INFLAMMATION AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 1)

items.assertion: T_H2 cytokines promote antifibrotic properties in SSc.
items.reason: They induce fibroblast activation and contribute to tissue remodeling.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; T_H2 cytokines actually promote fibrosis.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · INFLAMMATION AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 1)

items.assertion: Systemic sclerosis fibroblasts display an abnormally activated phenotype ex vivo.
items.reason: This is due to increased collagen production, spontaneous ROS generation, and constitutive expression of alpha smooth-muscle actin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why systemic sclerosis fibroblasts display an abnormally activated phenotype ex vivo.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · INFLAMMATION AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 2) | FIBROSIS

items.assertion: Endothelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EndoMT) does not contribute to perivascular fibrosis in systemic sclerosis.
items.reason: EndoMT leads to myofibroblast formation, which drives perivascular fibrosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because EndoMT contributes to perivascular fibrosis by giving rise to myofibroblasts.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · INFLAMMATION AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 2) | FIBROSIS

items.assertion: Patients with very early diagnosis of systemic sclerosis (VEDOSS) show progression to frank disease.
items.reason: These patients have puffy fingers, Raynaud's phenomenon, and SSc-specific autoantibodies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the presence of these features explains why VEDOSS progresses to frank disease.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · OVERVIEW | INITIAL CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.assertion: The presence of anti-centromere antibodies is a risk factor for significant interstitial lung disease in systemic sclerosis.
items.reason: Anti-centromere antibody-positive SSc patients have a lower risk of ILD.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason is true, as anti-centromere antibodies are associated with a lower risk of ILD.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PULMONARY FEATURES (part 1)

items.assertion: Surgical lung biopsy in systemic sclerosis is indicated for all patients to confirm interstitial lung disease.
items.reason: The histologic pattern on lung biopsy may predict the risk of progression of ILD, with NSIP having a better prognosis than UIP.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; surgical lung biopsy is indicated only in patients with atypical findings on chest imaging.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · PULMONARY FEATURES (part 1)

items.assertion: Severe erosive esophagitis may be found in patients with minimal symptoms.
items.reason: Esophageal manometry shows abnormal motility in most SSc patients, even in the absence of symptoms.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why severe erosive esophagitis may be found with minimal symptoms.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · GASTROINTESTINAL INVOLVEMENT

items.assertion: Primary biliary cirrhosis is common in patients with SSc.
items.reason: The liver is rarely affected in patients with SSc, but primary biliary cirrhosis may occur, particularly in those with limited cutaneous disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the passage states that the liver is rarely affected.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · GASTROINTESTINAL INVOLVEMENT

items.subtopic: DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES: IMMUNOMODULATORY AGENTS | TABLE 372-7 Key Principles in Management
items.assertion: Methotrexate has a significant effect on SSc skin involvement.
items.reason: Methotrexate had only modest effect on SSc skin involvement in small studies.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; methotrexate's effect was only modest.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES: IMMUNOMODULATORY AGENTS | TABLE 372-7 Key Principles in Management

items.subtopic: DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES: IMMUNOMODULATORY AGENTS | TABLE 372-7 Key Principles in Management
items.assertion: ACE inhibitors are effective in reducing the frequency or severity of Raynaud's episodes.
items.reason: While ACE inhibitors do not reduce the frequency or severity of episodes, angiotensin II receptor blockers such as losartan are effective and well tolerated.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; ACE inhibitors are not effective for Raynaud's episodes.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES: IMMUNOMODULATORY AGENTS | TABLE 372-7 Key Principles in Management

items.subtopic: LOCALIZED SCLERODERMA | MIXED CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE
items.assertion: Mixed connective tissue disease is characterized by features of systemic sclerosis.
items.reason: Patients with mixed connective tissue disease have high titers of autoantibodies to U1-RNP and may present with sclerodactyly over time.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the presence of features like sclerodactyly in MCTD is linked to its association with systemic sclerosis.
items.reference: Systemic Sclerosis Scleroderma and Related Disorders · LOCALIZED SCLERODERMA | MIXED CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE
